output_parsed.mathematical_role: First-order rate constant governing conversion of immature APCs (APC) to mature APCs (mAPC) under maximally pro-maturational stimulation.
output_parsed.study_overview: This parameter quantifies the maximum maturation rate of human antigen-presenting cells (APCs), relevant for how quickly tumor-site APCs can acquire mature phenotype and present antigen. We use reported durations for terminal dendritic cell (DC) maturation under strong cytokine cocktails to infer a first-order maturation rate constant.
output_parsed.study_design: Primary human monocyte-derived DC studies reported explicit culture durations for terminal maturation following differentiation, typically 24–48 hours under a TNF-α/IL-1β/IL-6/PGE2 cocktail. We extract verbatim time statements from independent studies and propagate uncertainty via bootstrap and parametric Monte Carlo to estimate the maximal first-order maturation rate (1/day).

inputs.description: Terminal maturation duration alternative (upper end)
inputs.value_snippet: "While in culture, immature cells were partially or terminally matured by exposure to inflammatory cytokines for 24 or 48 hours, respectively."

inputs.value_snippet: "86.4 ks (24 h): The length of one day of Earth by standard."

inputs.description: Lower bound for fraction of maturation considered 'complete' in time statements (assumption)

inputs.description: Upper bound for fraction of maturation considered 'complete' (assumption)

key_assumptions.text: Terminal maturation durations (24–48 h) reported under strong cytokine cocktails represent near-maximal human APC maturation rates applicable to the tumor microenvironment when pro-maturational signals are saturating.
APC maturation can be approximated as a first-order process with respect to immature APCs, so the time to reach a given completion fraction p follows t = −ln(1−p)/k.
Text statements such as “mature within 48 h” imply achieving between 90% and 99% of the mature phenotype; we model p ~ Uniform[0.90, 0.99] to encode this uncertainty.
Reported maturation times have protocol/measurement variability on the order of a couple of hours; we model this as Gaussian noise with SD = 2 h, truncated to positive times.
Unit conversion from hours to days is exact (1 day = 24 h), so no additional conversion uncertainty is introduced.

output_parsed.derivation_explanation:
1) Data assembly: We extracted explicit terminal maturation durations from independent human DC studies: (a) FastDC protocol uses “activation … for another 24 h,” and (b) a methods paper states terminal maturation by inflammatory cytokines occurs over “24 or 48 hours.” We treat these as empirical candidates for maximal maturation time under strong stimulation. citeturn2view0turn5view0

2) Kinetic model (ASSUMPTION 2): We approximate maturation as first-order with respect to immature APCs. For a target completion fraction p, the time to complete is t = −ln(1−p)/k, hence k = −ln(1−p)/t. This connects reported maturation times to a rate constant. citeturn9search12

3) Uncertainty in what “complete” means (ASSUMPTION 3): Because text phrases rarely define an exact threshold, we sample p uniformly between 0.90 and 0.99 per draw, spanning common practice for “near-complete” phenotypic maturation.

4) Bootstrap and measurement variability (ASSUMPTION 4): Each Monte Carlo draw bootstraps one reported time with replacement and adds Gaussian noise (SD 2 h) to reflect protocol variability. We then convert hours to days using 24 h/day (ASSUMPTION 5). citeturn7search13

5) Compute rate draws: For each draw, we compute k = −ln(1−p)/t_days. After 20,000 draws, we summarize with median, IQR, and 95% percentile interval to provide outlier-robust statistics (Monte Carlo/percentile bootstrap framework). citeturn8search5
output_parsed.key_study_limitations:
- In vitro/ex vivo context: Human monocyte-derived DCs matured in vitro may not fully capture in vivo tumor-site APC maturation dynamics, especially under immunosuppressive cues; therefore, results reflect a maximal rate rather than typical in-tumor rates.

- Protocol heterogeneity: Cytokine cocktails and culture conditions vary across labs, contributing to time variability not fully captured by simple Gaussian noise.

- Phenotypic endpoint ambiguity: Textual reports do not specify an exact maturation fraction threshold; our Uniform[0.90, 0.99] prior may misrepresent the true criterion in any particular study.

- APC subset diversity: moDCs and LCs differ in maturation kinetics; pooling 24 h and 48 h endpoints across subtypes may over- or under-estimate k for specific APC subsets.

primary_data_sources.title: Mature Dendritic Cells Derived from Human Monocytes Within 48 Hours: A Novel Strategy for Dendritic Cell Differentiation from Blood Precursors
Development of a new protocol for 2-day generation of mature dendritic cells from human monocytes

Langerhans-type and monocyte-derived human dendritic cells have different susceptibilities to mRNA electroporation with distinct effects on maturation and activation: implications for immunogenicity in dendritic cell-based immunotherapy

methodological_sources.title: Exponential decay (encyclopedic reference)
methodological_sources.used_for: First-order kinetics relation t = −ln(1−p)/k and link to exponential time constant.
methodological_sources.method_description: Provides the differential equation for first-order decay and closed-form solution used to relate time to rate constant.

methodological_sources.title: The Bootstrap Method for Assessing Statistical Accuracy
methodological_sources.used_for: Nonparametric bootstrap/percentile intervals to quantify uncertainty from limited measurements.
methodological_sources.method_description: Classic methodological paper describing bootstrap resampling to obtain distributional estimates and confidence intervals.

species_match.justification: All primary data are from human donors (moDCs/LCs).

system_match.justification: Ex vivo maturation of primary human cells under defined cytokines approximates in vivo responses but lacks tissue context.

overall_confidence.justification: Multiple independent studies concord on 24–48 h terminal maturation; however, endpoints are textual and protocols differ.

indication_match.justification: Immune-oncology setting modeled; studies are general human DCs not isolated from tumors.

regimen_match.justification: Strong cytokine cocktails emulate pro-maturational signals but are not the exact tumor regimen; used to estimate maximum rate.

biomarker_population_match.justification: Mixed APC subtypes (moDCs and LCs) with maturation markers (CD83, HLA-DR), partially aligned with model APC definition.

stage_burden_match.justification: No cancer stage/burden context in the in vitro studies.

output_parsed.mathematical_role: Rate constant for net cancer cell proliferation in the logistic tumor growth term V_T.C1*(1 - C_total/C_max).
output_parsed.study_overview: This parameter quantifies the intrinsic cancer growth rate (per day) needed for the tumor logistic growth term. We estimate it from human in vivo tumor volume doubling times (VDT) measured on serial clinical imaging across multiple solid tumors, then convert VDT to a growth rate using the exponential growth relation.
output_parsed.study_design: Primary data come from human, pre-treatment longitudinal imaging studies that report tumor volume doubling times in days for NSCLC, HCC, melanoma, and RCC. We combine study-level summary statistics using a Monte Carlo mixture model that propagates uncertainty in reported medians/ranges and the VDT-to-rate conversion.

parameter_estimates.derivation_code:
import math
import random
import statistics

def _lognormal_params_from_range(median_val, min_val, max_val, p_low=0.10, p_high=0.90):
    """
    Estimate lognormal parameters (mu, sigma) from a median and an assumed central range.
    We assume min ≈ p_low percentile and max ≈ p_high percentile of the distribution.
    Returns (mu, sigma) for lognormal where median = exp(mu).
    """
    mu = math.log(median_val)
    # avoid degenerate cases
    min_val = max(min_val, 1e-9)
    max_val = max(max_val, min_val * (1.0 + 1e-6))
    z_low = 0.0
    z_high = 0.0
    # inverse normal quantiles for p_low and p_high
    import math as _m
    # approximate inverse CDF (Acklam's method) to avoid external deps
    def norm_ppf(p):
        # coefficients from Peter John Acklam's approximation
        a1=-39.69683028665376; a2=220.9460984245205; a3=-275.9285104469687
        a4=138.3577518672690; a5=-30.66479806614716; a6=2.506628277459239
        b1=-54.47609879822406; b2=161.5858368580409; b3=-155.6989798598866
        b4=66.80131188771972; b5=-13.28068155288572
        c1=-0.007784894002430293; c2=-0.3223964580411365; c3=-2.400758277161838
        c4=-2.549732539343734; c5=4.374664141464968; c6=2.938163982698783
        d1=0.007784695709041462; d2=0.3224671290700398; d3=2.445134137142996
        d4=3.754408661907416
        plow=0.02425; phigh=1-plow
        if p<plow:
            q=math.sqrt(-2*math.log(p))
            return (((((c1*q+c2)*q+c3)*q+c4)*q+c5)*q+c6)/((((d1*q+d2)*q+d3)*q+d4)*q+1)
        if p>phigh:
            q=math.sqrt(-2*math.log(1-p))
            return -(((((c1*q+c2)*q+c3)*q+c4)*q+c5)*q+c6)/((((d1*q+d2)*q+d3)*q+d4)*q+1)
        q=p-0.5; r=q*q
        return (((((a1*r+a2)*r+a3)*r+a4)*r+a5)*r+a6)*q/((((((b1*r+b2)*r+b3)*r+b4)*r+b5)*r)+1)
    z_low = norm_ppf(p_low)
    z_high = norm_ppf(p_high)
    # solve for sigma using log-quantiles
    log_min = math.log(min_val)
    log_max = math.log(max_val)
    # log_min = mu + sigma*z_low ; log_max = mu + sigma*z_high -> sigma = (log_max - log_min)/(z_high - z_low)
    sigma = (log_max - log_min)/max(1e-9, (z_high - z_low))
    return mu, max(1e-6, sigma)

def derive_parameter(inputs):
    # Normalize inputs if a dict containing 'inputs' was passed
    try:
        if isinstance(inputs, dict) and 'inputs' in inputs:
            inputs = inputs['inputs']
    except Exception:
        pass
    # Extract inputs
    rng_seed = next((x['value'] for x in inputs if x['name']=='random_seed'), 17)
    n_iter = int(next((x['value'] for x in inputs if x['name']=='n_iter'), 20000))
    random.seed(rng_seed)
    ln2 = math.log(2.0)

    # Build study models (lognormal for DT)
    studies = []
    # NSCLC (single CT-GTV DT; put an uncertainty prior on sigma)
    dt_nsclc = next(x['value'] for x in inputs if x['name']=='DT_NSCLC_CT_GTV_median')
    # assume median; prior on sigma as lognormal with median GSD=1.5 and 95% between 1.2 and 2.0
    # implement by drawing sigma from Normal(mu=ln(1.5), sd=0.2) in log-GSD space each iteration
    studies.append({'type':'median_only','median':dt_nsclc,'log_gsd_mu':math.log(1.5),'log_gsd_sd':0.2})

    # HCC (median with range)
    hcc_med = next(x['value'] for x in inputs if x['name']=='DT_HCC_median')
    hcc_min = next(x['value'] for x in inputs if x['name']=='DT_HCC_min')
    hcc_max = next(x['value'] for x in inputs if x['name']=='DT_HCC_max')
    mu_hcc, sigma_hcc = _lognormal_params_from_range(hcc_med, hcc_min, hcc_max, 0.10, 0.90)
    studies.append({'type':'median_range','mu':mu_hcc,'sigma':sigma_hcc})

    # Melanoma primary (median with range)
    mel_med = next(x['value'] for x in inputs if x['name']=='DT_Melanoma_primary_median')
    mel_min = next(x['value'] for x in inputs if x['name']=='DT_Melanoma_primary_min')
    mel_max = next(x['value'] for x in inputs if x['name']=='DT_Melanoma_primary_max')
    mu_mel, sigma_mel = _lognormal_params_from_range(mel_med, mel_min, mel_max, 0.10, 0.90)
    studies.append({'type':'median_range','mu':mu_mel,'sigma':sigma_mel})

    # RCC (mean as proxy for central tendency; plus range)
    rcc_c = next(x['value'] for x in inputs if x['name']=='DT_RCC_mean_as_proxy_median')
    rcc_min = next(x['value'] for x in inputs if x['name']=='DT_RCC_min')
    rcc_max = next(x['value'] for x in inputs if x['name']=='DT_RCC_max')
    # treat reported mean as approximate median for central tendency (bias addressed via extra uncertainty inflation)
    mu_rcc, sigma_rcc = _lognormal_params_from_range(rcc_c, rcc_min, rcc_max, 0.10, 0.90)
    studies.append({'type':'median_range','mu':mu_rcc,'sigma':sigma_rcc, 'inflate_sigma':1.15})

    draws = []
    for _ in range(n_iter):
        st = random.choice(studies)
        if st['type']=='median_only':
            # sample sigma via prior on GSD, then lognormal draw around the median
            log_gsd = random.gauss(st['log_gsd_mu'], st['log_gsd_sd'])
            sigma = max(1e-6, log_gsd)
            mu = math.log(st['median'])
        else:
            sigma = st['sigma']
            # multiplicative noise to reflect uncertainty in mapping range->percentiles
            sigma *= random.lognormvariate(0.0, 0.10)
            if 'inflate_sigma' in st:
                sigma *= st['inflate_sigma']
            mu = st['mu']
        # draw DT from lognormal with parameters (mu, sigma)
        dt = math.exp(random.gauss(mu, sigma))
        # convert to per-day growth rate
        k = ln2 / dt
        draws.append(k)

    # robust summaries
    draws_sorted = sorted(draws)
    n = len(draws_sorted)
    def pct(q):
        idx = int(q*(n-1))
        return draws_sorted[idx]
    median_param = pct(0.5)
    q1 = pct(0.25)
    q3 = pct(0.75)
    iqr_param = q3 - q1
    ci95_param = [pct(0.025), pct(0.975)]
    return {
        'median_param': median_param,
        'iqr_param': iqr_param,
        'ci95_param': ci95_param,
        'median': median_param,
        'iqr': iqr_param,
        'ci95': ci95_param
    }

key_assumptions.text: VDT-to-rate conversion follows exponential growth over the short interscan intervals used in the primary studies, so k = ln(2)/VDT applies to tumor volume dynamics.
Reported VDT ranges approximate central 80% variability (10th–90th percentiles); this allows estimation of a lognormal dispersion (sigma) from published min–max values.

When only a single central VDT is reported (e.g., NSCLC CT-GTV), the between-patient dispersion in VDT follows a lognormal distribution with geometric standard deviation (GSD) centered at 1.5 (95% roughly 1.2–2.0).
Across solid tumors, a generic cancer growth rate for the model can be represented by an equal-weight mixture over tumor types (NSCLC, HCC, melanoma, RCC) to capture cross-indication heterogeneity.
Using a reported mean VDT (RCC) as proxy for the median introduces bias; we mitigate this by inflating the dispersion by 15% during uncertainty propagation.

output_parsed.derivation_explanation:
Step 1: Gather human in vivo VDTs from independent studies (NSCLC CT-GTV 139 days; HCC median 117 days, range 29–398; primary cutaneous melanoma median 94 days, range 50–377; RCC mean ≈460 days, range 174–913). These are extracted from abstracts and expressed in days. [ASSUMPTION 4]

Step 2: Convert VDT to a per-day exponential growth rate using k = ln(2)/VDT. This follows standard exponential growth theory and is explicitly stated as T = (ln 2)/k (so k = ln 2 / T). [ASSUMPTION 1]

Step 3: For studies reporting a central value and a range, approximate that the reported min and max correspond to the 10th and 90th percentiles of a lognormal VDT distribution. Solve for the lognormal sigma from these percentiles, with small multiplicative noise (lognormal SD 0.10) to reflect percentile mapping uncertainty. [ASSUMPTION 2]

Step 4: For the NSCLC CT-GTV study that reports a single DT without a spread, place a prior on between-patient dispersion: log-GSD ~ Normal(ln 1.5, 0.2). Each Monte Carlo draw samples a sigma from this prior, then draws a VDT from a lognormal centered at the reported median. [ASSUMPTION 3]

Step 5: For the RCC study that reports a mean, treat it as an approximate median and inflate sigma by 15% to hedge potential central-tendency bias. [ASSUMPTION 5]

Step 6: Perform Monte Carlo sampling (n_iter) by repeatedly (a) selecting one study at random with equal weight to represent generic solid tumor behavior, (b) sampling a VDT from that study’s lognormal distribution, and (c) converting to k = ln(2)/VDT. Aggregate draws into robust summaries: median, IQR, and 95% percentile interval.
output_parsed.key_study_limitations:
- Heterogeneous tumor indications: Combining NSCLC, HCC, melanoma, and RCC increases generality but reduces indication specificity; true growth for a given tumor type may deviate materially from the generic estimate.

- Study-level (not individual-level) data: Many sources provide only medians and ranges; reconstructing dispersion from ranges (and assuming lognormality) introduces model dependence that may under- or over-estimate uncertainty.

- Potential bias in RCC central tendency: Using a study’s mean VDT as a proxy for median can bias the central estimate; we partially mitigate by inflating dispersion but residual bias may remain.

- Exponential approximation: Short-interval exponential fits are reasonable pre-treatment, but longer-term growth can become sub-exponential (e.g., Gompertzian); our use of k = ln2/VDT assumes local exponential behavior.

- Imaging and contour variability: Differences in imaging modality and volumetry (CT-GTV, PET-MTV, ultrasound) may introduce systematic differences in VDT estimates across studies.

primary_data_sources.title: Natural growth and disease progression of non-small cell lung cancer evaluated with 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose PET/CT
Growth rate of asymptomatic hepatocellular carcinoma and its clinical implications
Tumor doubling time of cutaneous melanoma and its metastasis
Tumor doubling time of renal cell carcinoma measured by CT

methodological_sources.title: Exponential growth and doubling time (Calculus text)
methodological_sources.method_description: Provides the derivation and units for the exponential growth model P(t)=P0 e^{kt}, showing that doubling time T=(ln 2)/k and hence k=ln(2)/T with units of 1/time.

species_match.justification: All primary data are from human clinical cohorts with serial imaging.

system_match.justification: In vivo tumor growth observed in patients prior to treatment.

overall_confidence.justification: Multiple independent studies with consistent methodology, but reliance on study-level summaries and cross-indication pooling reduces precision.

indication_match.justification: Model uses a generic solid tumor growth rate; sources span several solid tumor types (adjacent rather than exact match).

regimen_match.justification: Measurements reflect natural, pre-treatment growth, matching baseline tumor kinetics in the model.

biomarker_population_match.justification: No stratification by specific biomarkers relevant to the model is available in the extracted values.

stage_burden_match.justification: Mixture of localized and regionally advanced tumors; partially overlapping with typical QSP IO baseline populations.
